issues.claim: Load-bearing FIXME on a core path: the comment at lines 145-147 states containsDeferredPromptValues only inspects own data properties, so deferred prompt values hidden behind private fields or method-only wrapper DTOs are missed (links https://github.com/dahlia/optique/issues/407). This function is the membership test that gates the whole strip mechanism feeding stripDeferredPromptValues (line 567), which scrubs the parsed value before it is handed to the user-supplied load()/getConfigPath(). A documented known correctness gap on a core path -> likely IF-4.

Load-bearing FIXME on a core path: the comment at lines 190-192 states this Proxy changes method-receiver semantics and still cannot scrub deferred prompt values hidden behind private fields or wrapper methods (same issue #407). It is reached from stripDeferredPromptValues (line 271) on the same parsed-value scrub path that runs before load()/getConfigPath(). Documented unresolved defect on a core path -> likely IF-4.

Comment contradicts adjacent code: lines 547-548 say Phase 1 should 'mark the context as unresolved so prompt(bindConfig(...)) can defer interactive fallback,' but the adjacent statement (if (parsed === undefined) return {};) returns EMPTY annotations and marks nothing. The actual unresolved-marking is performed by a different method, [phase1ConfigAnnotationsKey] (lines 534-541, returns { [contextId]: phase1ConfigAnnotationMarker }). The comment describes behavior the code beside it does not perform -> likely IF-4 (flag-not-assert; comment may intend the cross-method protocol).

suppressed.reason: catch { configValue = undefined; } in getConfigOrDefault swallows the accessor error and falls through to the default. Suppressed: this is the documented and explicitly tested contract -- index.test.ts:985-1008 ('key accessor function that throws falls through to default') asserts exactly this, and the priority order config > default means a throwing accessor is intended to yield the default, not surface to the caller.

catch (error) in single-file getConfigPath mode is non-empty and discriminating: ENOENT returns {} (documented 'missing config file is optional'), SyntaxError is rethrown wrapped with the path, all other errors are rethrown. Errors reach the caller; no swallow.

activeConfigRegistry / activeConfigMetaRegistry are module-level maps written by setActiveConfig/setActiveConfigMeta and buildAnnotations, but writes are coordinated by per-instance contextId keys and cleared by Symbol.dispose; the cross-package deferredPromptValueKey (Symbol.for) is only read here, never written. No second uncoordinated writer of shared state.

All symbols imported from ./index.ts by both test files (bindConfig, createConfigContext, getActiveConfig, getActiveConfigMeta, setActiveConfig, setActiveConfigMeta, ConfigLoadResult, ConfigMeta) are real exports of index.ts. No import-vs-export breach.

readFileSync reads a config path produced by the application's own getConfigPath; this is a local CLI config-parsing library with no resource handlers, DB queries, tenant/owner model, or sibling guarded path. No authorization surface exists, so IF-1 is not applicable.

result.consumed used as .length (line 811, success branch = array) and as > 0 (line 829, failure branch = number) is correct narrowing over the @optique/core ParserResult discriminated union, not a name-that-lies or contradiction.

The Symbol.for keys (deferPromptUntilConfigResolvesKey, phase1ConfigAnnotationsKey, etc.) are single-definition symbols, not a named enum/status/allowed-value set duplicated across 2+ places. No second divergent definition exists to compare against.

issues.claim: Module-level interface-contract breach: `from app.bot import bot` imports a symbol `bot` that app/bot.py does NOT export. In app/bot.py the names `bot` (line 97) and `dp` (line 112) are LOCAL variables inside setup_bot(); there is no module-level `bot`/`dp` (verified by AST + grep — `^(bot|dp)\s*=` has zero matches). This import raises ImportError at runtime when _send_cloudpayments_success_notification runs (a payment-success notification path). The very next line `if not bot:` shows the author expected an importable (possibly-None) module global that does not exist.

Same interface-contract breach as line 353, in _send_cloudpayments_fail_notification: `from app.bot import bot` imports a non-existent module-level symbol (app/bot.py defines `bot` only as a local inside setup_bot()). Reaching this payment-failure notification helper raises ImportError.

Interface-contract breach: `from app.bot import dp` inside handle_successful_topup_with_cart imports a symbol `dp` that app/bot.py does not export (`dp` is a local variable in setup_bot(), line 112; no module-level `dp`). The function then dereferences `dp.storage` (line 495), so reaching this cart-completion path after a successful top-up raises ImportError.

Code contradicts its own documented rule / load-bearing TODO on a core security path. The comment at lines 172-173 says signature verification is 'temporarily disabled for debugging / TODO: re-enable after configuring MulenPay'. The handler calls _verify_mulenpay_signature (line 174) but on failure only logs a warning ('processing anyway for debugging', line 176) and the rejecting `return ...401` is commented out (line 179), so a MulenPay webhook with an INVALID signature is still processed into the balance/payment tables. The sibling handler webserver/payments.py:245-249 returns 401 on the same failure — confirming this deviates from the intended contract.

Silent cross-component coupling: two independent webhook server implementations write the same payment/balance business resource via the same callback (process_mulenpay_callback) with no coordinated contract — app/webserver/payments.py:245-263 (FastAPI, mounted in the unified app) and this app/external/webhook_server.py (aiohttp). They diverge on the security contract (the FastAPI one rejects bad signatures; this one processes them anyway), so whichever is deployed silently determines whether MulenPay callbacks are authenticated. The duplicated/uncoordinated second writer (external/webhook_server.py) also handles Tribute (line 357) and CryptoBot (line 401) the same way.

Missing authorization on a read endpoint while the sibling path guards it. cabinet download_media (GET /media/{file_id}, lines 148-151) has NO auth dependency — it streams ANY Telegram file by file_id with the bot token, no get_current_cabinet_user. The upload sibling in the same file (line 57) requires get_current_cabinet_user, and the equivalent webapi download (app/webapi/routes/media.py:120) requires Security(require_api_token). Impact is bounded because Telegram file_ids are opaque/hard to guess, but the operation is an unauthenticated bot-token-backed file fetch where every sibling op is authenticated.

suppressed.reason: No module-level import cycle exists in scope. Built the full module-level import graph over all 423 app modules (AST, top-level Import/ImportFrom only) and ran Tarjan SCC: zero SCCs of size>1 and zero self-loops. Cross-module cycles (e.g. bot<->handlers) are broken via function-local imports (the documented non-defect form), so there is no module-level back-edge to report.

Considered: UserStatus here (ACTIVE/DISABLED/LIMITED/EXPIRED) vs app/database/models.py:85 UserStatus (ACTIVE/BLOCKED/DELETED) share the name with divergent membership — the literal IF-6 shape. Suppressed: they model two distinct systems (RemnaWave panel state vs local DB user lifecycle) and the developers deliberately disambiguate them: app/cabinet/routes/admin_users.py imports the panel one as `UserStatus as PanelUserStatus` (lines 165, 1668) specifically to avoid the collision. Firing here would be a false positive (intentional distinct domains, not accidental drift of the same set).

WheelPrizeType (models.py:144) and WheelPrizeType (app/cabinet/schemas/wheel.py:19) have the SAME name and IDENTICAL members (SUBSCRIPTION_DAYS, BALANCE_BONUS, TRAFFIC_GB, PROMOCODE, NOTHING). Empty symmetric difference -> never fire. Likewise SubscriptionStatus/SubscriptionStatusEnum and UserStatus/UserStatusEnum have differing NAMES (not the same named set) and/or identical members.

False positive from static export analysis: `from app.services import payment_service` imports the SUBMODULE app/services/payment_service.py (file exists), not a top-level name. Same for the other `from app.services import payment_service` sites (mulenpay/pal24/platega/wata/yookassa) and `from app.handlers import common/menu/...` in app/bot.py (all resolve to existing submodules).

Empty `except Exception: pass` guards an optional, best-effort column reset (`if hasattr(ticket, 'last_sla_reminder_at'): ticket.last_sla_reminder_at = None`). The documented contract is a no-op when the column is absent; the error must not reach the caller. Suppressed per flag-don't-assert (default-is-documented-contract).

Bare `except: pass` wraps a cosmetic Telegram message.edit_text retry inside an already-logged outer handler. Best-effort UI operation; swallowing is the intended contract (do not crash the /start flow on a failed edit). Same applies to the other start.py best-effort UI swallows (805, 895, 919, 984) and the channel_checker/message_patch/photo_message Telegram-API retries.

delete_device (DELETE /devices/{hwid}) takes a client id but is properly owner-scoped: the RemnaWave delete payload pins userUuid=user.remnawave_uuid (line 2827) from the JWT-authenticated user, so the hwid can only target the caller's own devices. Not an unscoped lookup-by-id.

MiniApp mutation endpoints (remove_connected_device, update autopay/devices/traffic/servers, purchase/renew, claim promo, etc.) resolve the user from HMAC-validated Telegram initData via parse_webapp_init_data + get_user_by_telegram_id (_resolve_user_from_init_data / _authorize_miniapp_user), not from a client-supplied user_id. Identity is cryptographically bound (parse_webapp_init_data verifies the WebAppData HMAC and rejects on mismatch), and all ops self-scope to that user. Properly authorized.

issues.claim: Module-level circular dependency. app/handlers/admin/main.py:12 does a module-level `from app.handlers.admin import support_settings`, importing the admin package; the package __init__ (app/handlers/admin/__init__.py:2-38, line 11 = `main`) does `from . import (... main ...)` at module level. So importing main triggers the package __init__ which re-imports main, and importing the package triggers main which imports the package. This is a genuine module-level back-edge (Tarjan SCC over the whole app import graph found exactly this 2-node cycle). Partial-initialization hazard depending on which module is loaded first.

Interface contract breach: imports `dp` from app.bot, but app/bot.py never exports `dp` at module level. `dp` is a LOCAL variable created inside setup_bot() (app/bot.py:112) and is never assigned to a module global nor declared `global`. app.bot's only top-level names are logger, debug_callback_handler, setup_bot, shutdown_bot. This `from app.bot import dp` (a function-local lazy import inside handle_successful_topup_with_cart) will raise ImportError whenever that code path runs.

Interface contract breach: imports `bot` from app.bot, but app/bot.py exports no module-level `bot`. `bot` is a LOCAL variable created inside setup_bot() (app/bot.py:97), never a module global. The code then does `if not bot: return` (line 356) as if app.bot.bot were a module singleton — but the import itself would raise ImportError before that guard, so the success-notification path is broken whenever invoked.

Interface contract breach (second site): `from app.bot import bot` in the failure-notification helper, but app.bot has no module-level `bot` (it is local to setup_bot() at app/bot.py:97). ImportError at runtime when the failure-notification path executes; the following `if not bot: return` (line 406) is unreachable because the import raises first.

Code contradicts the adjacent documented intent. The comment at line 172 ('Временно отключаем проверку подписи для отладки' = temporarily disable signature check for debugging) and the load-bearing TODO at line 173 ('Включить обратно...' = re-enable after configuring MulenPay) say the signature MUST be verified, but on verification failure the handler only logs a warning ('processing anyway for debugging', line 176) and falls through to process the webhook; the rejecting `return ... 401` is commented out at line 179. A forged MulenPay payment callback is accepted. The sibling MulenPay handler app/webserver/payments.py:245-249 correctly returns 401 on the same check, confirming the intended contract.

Name/behavior lie + contract violation. The balance-prize branch does `user.balance += amount` (line 124) and logs/returns 'Balance increased by {amount}' (lines 128,129), but the User model has NO `balance` attribute/property/setter — only balance_kopeks (column, models.py:974), balance_rubles (read-only property, 1035), and add_balance(kopeks) (1087). Assigning a phantom `balance` never credits the spendable balance (the += also reads a nonexistent attr -> AttributeError). It also treats prize_value as float RUBLES (line 116 `float(prize_value)`) whereas the canonical awarder uses integer KOPEKS. The contest winner's balance prize is silently lost / errors despite the success message.

Silent cross-component coupling: two uncoordinated writers of the same business resource (user balance, for the same contest-prize concept) with divergent contracts. The canonical writer app/services/contests/attempt_service.py:301-303 interprets prize_value as kopeks and writes `user.balance_kopeks += kopeks`; the cabinet copy app/cabinet/routes/contests.py:116-124 interprets prize_value as float rubles and writes a nonexistent `user.balance`. The cabinet route is the second, divergent, uncoordinated writer — same prize semantics, incompatible units and target field.

Read of a protected resource with no authorization while siblings have one. cabinet download_media (GET /media/{file_id}) declares no auth dependency, so any unauthenticated caller who has a Telegram file_id can fetch ticket-attachment media. The sibling upload_media in the same file requires Depends(get_current_cabinet_user) (line 60), and the parallel webapi twin app/webapi/routes/media.py:119-122 protects its download with Security(require_api_token). Flag-don't-assert: may be a deliberate public image-serving endpoint (cache headers, used for tags), but the asymmetry vs both siblings is suspicious.

suppressed.reason: GAME_* constants (lines 26-32) are derived directly from GameType enum values (e.g. GameType.QUEST_BUTTONS.value), so they are single-source-of-truth, not a divergent duplicate set.

UserStatus here (ACTIVE/DISABLED/LIMITED/EXPIRED, uppercase) models the external RemnaWave panel's status; models.py:85 UserStatus (active/blocked/deleted, lowercase) models the bot's local user status. Same class name but two distinct external/internal contracts (different domains) — not the same allowed-value set that must stay in sync.

UserStatusEnum {active,blocked,deleted} and SubscriptionStatusEnum {trial,active,expired,disabled,pending} are member-for-member IDENTICAL to models.UserStatus / models.SubscriptionStatus. No symmetric difference.

WheelPrizeType {subscription_days,balance_bonus,traffic_gb,promocode,nothing} and WheelPaymentType {telegram_stars,subscription_days} are identical to models.WheelPrizeType and models.WheelSpinPaymentType respectively. No divergence.

ButtonType/ButtonVisibility/ButtonOpenMode (menu-layout constructor subsystem) differ in membership from models.MainMenuButtonActionType/MainMenuButtonVisibility, but the class names are NOT the same and they belong to two distinct subsystems (menu_layout vs main_menu_button). IF-6 requires the SAME named set; suppressed as a different-name/different-domain judgment call.
PrizeType {days,balance,custom} (contest prizes) vs WheelPrizeType {subscription_days,balance_bonus,...} (wheel prizes) are different concepts/different names; not the same allowed-value set.

No statically-dead const-gated branches exist in scope: there are no module-level compile-time boolean flags gating if-arms, no `if True/False/0/1`, no `if __debug__`. Feature flags are runtime settings.* instance attributes, not compile-time constants — so no IF-10 raise.

Bare `except: pass` wraps a best-effort `await db.rollback()` during error cleanup (the outer error is already handled and a sentinel returned). Compensating cleanup; error is not meant to reach the caller. Same pattern at lines 1336 and 1532.
`except ValueError: pass` on list.remove(callback) in off() is intentional idempotent unsubscribe (item-not-present is a no-op). Documented contract; not a swallowed cross-boundary error.
`except StopAsyncIteration: pass` is the standard idiom for closing an async db-generator in a finally block; not an error swallow.

`except Exception: pass` only guards an optional UI button-choice computation (has_active_subscription) used to pick a message variant; default-to-False fallthrough is the intended contract, error need not reach caller.
`except Exception: pass` on a best-effort db.refresh of optional promo-group relationships, with an explicit alternate path immediately after; documented fallback, not a cross-boundary swallow.
get_payment_record is keyed only by (method,payment_id) but the route explicitly checks `record.user.id != user.id` (lines 1060, 1095) before returning/acting. Properly scoped; not unguarded.

All miniapp payment-status resolvers look up payments by id but each verifies `payment.user_id != user.id` (lines 1594,1660,1732,1822,1920,1995,2069,2134,2194) where user comes from HMAC-validated Telegram initData (_resolve_user_from_init_data, line 642 parse_webapp_init_data against BOT_TOKEN). Ownership-scoped.
get_poll_response_by_id is id-keyed but every handler checks `response.user_id != user.id` (lines 189,230,281). Scoped.
user.notification_settings (JSON column, models.py:1021) is written only by this cabinet route; no second uncoordinated writer found. Not IF-3a.

issues.claim: Cross-project IDOR on link-share deletion. The route is DELETE /projects/{project}/shares/{share}; `ID` binds to the `share` param and `ProjectID` binds to the `project` param. The permission gate `canDoLinkShare` (link_sharing_permissions.go:53) only checks CanWrite/IsAdmin on `share.ProjectID` (the project param), but the delete statement `s.Where("id = ?", share.ID).Delete(share)` deletes by `share.ID` ALONE with no `project_id = share.ProjectID` binding. A user who is admin/writer on project A can therefore delete a link share belonging to project B by passing project=A and share=<B's share id>. Sibling resources in the same package explicitly prevent this: TaskComment.getTaskCommentSimple and TaskAttachment.ReadOne add `task_id = ?` bindings with comments saying 'to prevent IDOR attacks', and ProjectView.Delete uses `id = ? AND project_id = ?`. Likely missing-scope defect.

ReadOne loads a link share by `id` only (`s.Where("id = ?", share.ID).Get(share)`) with no `project_id = share.ProjectID` binding, the same missing id<->project scope as Delete. Its permission gate CanRead (link_sharing_permissions.go:25) keys off `share.Hash` via GetProjectByShareHash rather than the share id/project, so the binding is not enforced in the CRUD method either. Lower certainty than the Delete case because the hash-based CanRead may incidentally block the by-id GET route when Hash is empty; flagged for the same id-not-scoped-to-project shape as the surrounding IDOR-hardened siblings.

Call passes the translation KEY where the language argument is expected: `i18n.T("notifications.task.overdue.overdue", getOverdueSinceString(until, n.User.Language))`. Every other i18n.T call in this file (and this method, e.g. lines 365-370) uses the `i18n.T(lang, key, ...args)` shape with `lang` first. Here `lang` is omitted, so the first positional arg becomes the (mistaken) lang and the actual key/args shift — the string will not be localized to `lang` and the substitution is malformed. Contradicts the convention demonstrated by the adjacent identical-shaped calls.

`exists, _ := tm.MembershipExists(s)` discards the error returned by MembershipExists (a DB existence check). On a DB error this silently treats the membership as non-existent and proceeds to tm.Create. Judgment-call swallow (intra-package helper, and a subsequent Create would surface a real DB fault), so flagged low; the contract of MembershipExists returns an error the caller drops.

suppressed.reason: (*TaskComment).Update updates by id with Cols('comment') and ignores auth, but the web.Permissions gate canUserModifyTaskComment (task_comment_permissions.go:30) enforces task write access AND authorship before the CRUD method runs. Established framework pattern; properly guarded upstream.
(*Label).Delete / Update operate by id with no in-method ownership scope, but CanUpdate/CanDelete (label_permissions.go:26-33) enforce isLabelOwner. Guarded by the permission layer.

(*Webhook).Delete/Update key off id only, but canDoWebhook (webhooks_permissions.go:47) loads the webhook by id, copies its real UserID/ProjectID, and checks ownership / project CanUpdate before the CRUD method. Bound correctly upstream.

Reaction Create/Delete force r.UserID = a.GetID() and key the row by user_id+entity, so a user can only affect their own reaction. CanRead/CanCreate/CanDelete additionally delegate to the underlying task. No IDOR.
(*Session).Delete is scoped `id = ? AND user_id = a.GetID()`, and CanDelete verifies session.UserID == auth id. Self-scoped, no IDOR.
CanUpdate returns true unconditionally, but the Update path (markTaskAsRead) is scoped `task_id = ? AND user_id = a.GetID()`, so it only clears the caller's own unread row. Inherently self-scoped.
(*APIToken).Delete is scoped `id = ? AND owner_id = a.GetID()`, and CanDelete checks token.OwnerID == auth id. Owner-scoped.

Project duplication copies user/team/link-share grants (incl. share Permission/Password) into the new project. This is the documented behavior of the duplicate endpoint and is gated by CanCreate (read on source + create on parent). Coordinated, not an uncoordinated second writer.

All files here are `package models` (a single Go package); Go files within one package do not import one another, so no intra-package import cycle is possible. Cross-package cycles cannot be evaluated because only pkg/models is in scope. No reportable module-level back-edge within scope.

Named enums/value-sets (RelationKind, Permission, ProjectViewKind, BucketConfigurationModeKind, SubscriptionEntityType, ReactionKind, SharingType, FavoriteKind, TaskRepeatMode, ReminderRelation) are each defined exactly once within scope. No duplicate definition with divergent membership.

Branches gated by config flags (e.g. config.WebhooksAllowNonRoutableIPs.GetBool(), MetricsEnabled.GetBool(), WebhooksEnabled.GetBool()) read runtime configuration via getter methods, not module-level compile-time constants, so no arm is statically dead.

issues.claim: The OpenAPI `enums` struct tag on BucketConfigurationMode is `enums:"none,manual,filter,manual"` — it lists `manual` twice. The authoritative Go const set (project_view.go:86-90 BucketConfigurationModeNone/Manual/Filter, and the UnmarshalJSON switch at lines 112-122) defines exactly three values {none, manual, filter}. The same named allowed-value set is therefore declared in two places with a non-empty symmetric difference (the tag has a redundant 4th element). The adjacent NOTE comment (project_view.go:82-84) explicitly requires keeping the enums tag in sync with the const values, so this is a real, documented-rule-violating drift in the generated API docs. Low severity (docs-only).

The code contradicts a DOCUMENTED rule stated in the immediately-preceding comment. project_view.go:82-84 says: 'When adding or changing enum values for BucketConfigurationModeKind, make sure to update the corresponding `enums` tag in the ProjectView struct to keep the OpenAPI documentation in sync.' The actual tag value `none,manual,filter,manual` (line 147) is not in sync with the three declared constants (none, manual, filter) — it carries a duplicate `manual` and thus does not faithfully reflect the enum membership the rule promises. Flagged as a likely doc/code contradiction (overlaps with the IF-6 raise on the same line; certainty MEDIUM since it is the doc-rule angle of the same defect).

suppressed.reason: TaskUnreadStatus.CanUpdate returns (true, nil) unconditionally — no ownership/authz check, unlike sibling permission methods that delegate to task.CanRead/CanUpdate. Considered as a missing-authz raise, but SUPPRESSED: the only operation it gates (Update -> markTaskAsRead, task_unread_statuses.go:51-60) deletes rows scoped by `task_id = ? AND user_id = a.GetID()`. The write is inherently scoped to the caller's own unread-status row, so there is no cross-user/IDOR impact even though the permission check is a no-op. No protected resource is exposed.

Load-bearing FIXME on a core path (Task.updateSingleTask): label updates are intentionally disabled inside the generic Update() because updateTaskLabels needs a user object for a per-label access check that the webhandler doesn't pass. Considered as an IF-4 load-bearing TODO/FIXME, but SUPPRESSED: it is an explained, deliberate design decision and is consistent with the documented contract (Task.Labels doc at tasks.go:80-81 says labels are read-only via Update and must be changed through the separate /tasks/{task}/labels endpoint, which performs the access check via canDoLabelTask/hasAccessToLabel). Not a contradiction.

Reaction.Create / Reaction.Delete (reaction.go:154,176) write/delete keyed only on user_id+entity_id without re-checking access inside the handler. SUPPRESSED: the web.Permissions contract invokes CanCreate/CanDelete first (reaction_permissions.go:55-61 -> getTask -> task.CanUpdate), which requires write access to the underlying task/comment's project; and the row is self-scoped to a.GetID(). Properly guarded.
